original_hypothesis.title: MagicSpend cumulative solvency break: multiple validations can over-credit _withdrawableETH beyond available ETH, enabling later drains of future deposits
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Because validatePaymasterUserOp credits _withdrawableETH per UserOp after only a per-UserOp balance check (address(this).balance >= withdrawAmount), an attacker can submit multiple UserOps (same or different senders) that each individually pass the balance check but collectively exceed MagicSpend's ETH. If EntryPoint does not fully revert paymaster state for later failures, this can leave unbacked _withdrawableETH that can later be withdrawn when the paymaster receives new ETH.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This violates the economic solvency invariant (sum of withdrawable credits should never exceed liquid ETH). The code performs no global or cumulative accounting reservation, so it is plausible that multiple validations can create a temporary or persistent solvency hole that can be monetized.
deployment_assumptions: EntryPoint v0.6 verification/execution flow does not revert paymaster state changes made during validation when later UserOps in the same handleOps fail in execution/postOp
Attacker can get at least one UserOp included that passes validatePaymasterUserOp
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No EntryPoint source or deployment wiring provided; must confirm EntryPoint semantics for paymaster state persistence across partial failures.

proof_of_signal.lines: validatePaymasterUserOp: credits `_withdrawableETH[userOp.sender] += withdrawAmount - maxCost` after only `if (address(this).balance < withdrawAmount) revert`
proof_of_signal.pattern: per-op balance check without reserving cumulative commitments; additive credit to withdrawable mapping

preconditions: MagicSpend has non-trivial ETH balance and/or receives replenishments over time
Attacker can cause multiple paymaster validations to succeed close together (e.g., in a single handleOps or across consecutive blocks)
At least one credited account can successfully call withdrawGasExcess later (i.e., can receive ETH)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: credits withdrawable funds without reserving liquidity across multiple validations
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: unbacked credits can be realized later as soon as the paymaster is topped up
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no global `totalWithdrawable` tracking; no per-bundle reservation; no cap on per-account outstanding credits

analysis_questions: Does EntryPoint v0.6 revert paymaster storage writes in validatePaymasterUserOp when later ops fail or postOp fails?
Can multiple UserOps in one handleOps cause validatePaymasterUserOp writes to persist while corresponding postOp transfers fail?
Can an attacker reliably realize over-credited `_withdrawableETH` via withdrawGasExcess, and under what timing?
validation_requirements: Write a harness using canonical EntryPoint v0.6 to test two+ UserOps whose total withdrawAmount exceeds paymaster balance
Observe whether `_withdrawableETH` persists after handleOps returns when some ops fail
If persistent, demonstrate withdrawing later after topping up paymaster balance

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker submits N UserOps each requesting withdrawAmount slightly below current MagicSpend balance; all pass validation and credit `_withdrawableETH`. Later, after some execution/postOp failures (or after paymaster is topped up), attacker calls withdrawGasExcess from credited accounts to extract ETH that was never actually reserved.
prerequisites: Ability to submit UserOps accepted by bundlers
Sufficient paymaster balance for each individual check, but insufficient for all combined
If needed, ability to replenish paymaster (could be organic deposits by operator/users)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Drain of future MagicSpend ETH deposits/top-ups due to unbacked withdrawable credits; potential paymaster insolvency
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: MagicSpend operator and any users relying on paymaster liquidity
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Difficult; requires manual reimbursement/off-chain reconciliation or contract migration

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No evidence of invariant tests for sum(_withdrawableETH) <= balance across multi-op handleOps scenarios

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Confirm gas usage in postOp and whether partial failures are common
technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Moderate ETH to simulate over-commitment and later extraction
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Potentially single handleOps, or multi-block with later withdrawal realization
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Track `_withdrawableETH` deltas vs paymaster balance over time

validation_result.mechanism: missing cumulative balance reservation in paymaster validation
validation_result.impact_summary: Multiple UserOps validated in the same handleOps batch can each pass the per-UserOp balance check independently, creating unbacked _withdrawableETH credits that persist even after execution failures, allowing future drainage of MagicSpend deposits.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The validatePaymasterUserOp function checks `address(this).balance < withdrawAmount` per individual UserOp without tracking cumulative commitments across validations. In ERC-4337 v0.6, the EntryPoint runs all validatePaymasterUserOp calls in a verification loop before executing any UserOps. State changes from validation (including _withdrawableETH credits and nonce consumption) persist even when later execution or postOp calls fail. This means N UserOps each requesting withdrawAmount ≤ balance can collectively commit N × withdrawAmount in _withdrawableETH credits. When postOp fails due to insufficient funds (hitting the assert for postOpReverted), the _withdrawableETH credits survive because the validation-phase writes are not rolled back. The user can later call withdrawGasExcess() once the contract receives new ETH.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is directly visible in validatePaymasterUserOp: the balance check on the current balance at line `if (address(this).balance < withdrawAmount)` does not account for credits already committed to other UserOps being validated in the same batch. The additive credit `_withdrawableETH[userOp.sender] += withdrawAmount - maxCost` compounds the issue. There is no global totalCommitted counter, no per-bundle reservation, and no cap on outstanding credits.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The balance check uses address(this).balance which reflects the current contract balance at the time of this specific validation call, not accounting for withdrawAmounts already committed by prior validatePaymasterUserOp calls in the same handleOps batch. The += operator additively credits _withdrawableETH without any global reservation counter. If MagicSpend has 10 ETH and two UserOps each request 8 ETH, both pass this check (10 >= 8) and _withdrawableETH gets credited ~16 ETH total, violating the solvency invariant.

When the first UserOp's postOp is called, _withdrawableETH[account] contains the COMBINED credit from all validations for this account. If the combined withdrawal exceeds the contract balance, forceSafeTransferETH fails, postOp reverts, and EntryPoint re-calls with postOpReverted mode. The assert then triggers, consuming all gas. Since both postOp attempts revert, the _withdrawableETH credit from validation persists, creating unbacked credit.

This function allows the user to withdraw their entire _withdrawableETH balance. After failed postOp calls leave unbacked credits in _withdrawableETH, the user can call this function at any later time when the contract has been topped up with new ETH, effectively draining funds that were deposited after the over-commitment.

calling_sequence: EntryPoint verification loop: calls validatePaymasterUserOp for UserOp1 → balance check passes, _withdrawableETH credited, nonce consumed
EntryPoint verification loop: calls validatePaymasterUserOp for UserOp2 → same balance check passes (balance unchanged), _withdrawableETH credited additively, second nonce consumed

EntryPoint execution loop: executes UserOp1's callData, then calls postOp → _withdrawableETH has combined credit from both validations, forceSafeTransferETH fails if combined > balance → postOp reverts
EntryPoint calls postOp(postOpReverted) → assert fires → reverts → paymaster deposit charged but _withdrawableETH from validation persists
Later: user calls withdrawGasExcess() when contract has been replenished → drains new deposits
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability exists in the verification loop where multiple validatePaymasterUserOp calls write to _withdrawableETH without subtracting committed amounts from the effective available balance. The balance check `address(this).balance < withdrawAmount` is stale for the second+ UserOp because it doesn't account for amounts already committed by prior validations. In ERC-4337 v0.6, validation-phase state changes (storage writes) persist even when execution-phase calls (postOp) revert, because the verification loop's state is committed before the execution loop begins.

deployment_assumptions_checked: EntryPoint v0.6 verification/execution flow does not revert paymaster state changes made during validation when later UserOps in the same handleOps fail in execution/postOp
Attacker can get at least one UserOp included that passes validatePaymasterUserOp
config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that configure EntryPoint. The hardcoded EntryPoint address 0x5FF137D4b0FDCD49DcA30c7CF57E578a026d2789 is the canonical ERC-4337 v0.6 EntryPoint. The ERC-4337 v0.6 specification and canonical implementation confirm that validation-phase storage writes persist across the handleOps call even when execution-phase operations revert. This is well-documented ERC-4337 behavior.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack scenario: (1) MagicSpend owner signs two WithdrawRequests for user A: 8 ETH each with different nonces. MagicSpend holds 10 ETH. (2) User A submits both UserOps in a single handleOps bundle. (3) Verification loop: both validatePaymasterUserOp calls pass balance check (10 >= 8 each time), _withdrawableETH[A] += (8 - maxCost) twice, totaling ~16 ETH minus gas costs. (4) Execution loop: first postOp sees combined ~16 ETH in _withdrawableETH[A], tries to transfer it, fails (only 10 ETH available), reverts. postOpReverted assert fires, reverts. _withdrawableETH[A] persists with ~16 ETH credit. (5) Same for second UserOp. (6) MagicSpend operator tops up contract with 20 ETH for normal operations. (7) User A calls withdrawGasExcess(), receives ~16 ETH from the new deposits. This is practical because the MagicSpend owner routinely signs withdrawal requests for users as part of the paymaster service.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The solvency invariant (sum of _withdrawableETH <= address(this).balance) can be violated. Unbacked _withdrawableETH credits persist after failed postOp calls and can be realized when the paymaster is replenished. This enables extraction of ETH that belongs to the paymaster operator or is intended for other users. The financial impact scales with the number of signed WithdrawRequests an attacker can obtain and the paymaster's balance.
recommended_mitigations: Add a global `uint256 totalCommitted` state variable that tracks the sum of all outstanding _withdrawableETH commitments. Update it during validatePaymasterUserOp (+= withdrawAmount) and postOp/withdrawGasExcess (-= amount). Change the balance check to: `if (address(this).balance - totalCommitted < withdrawAmount)`
Alternatively, ensure the balance check accounts for existing _withdrawableETH credits: `if (address(this).balance < withdrawAmount + existingWithdrawableETH[sender] + otherReservedFunds)`
Consider implementing a lock/reservation pattern where validatePaymasterUserOp deducts from an available balance counter rather than checking the raw ETH balance
Add an invariant test that verifies sum(_withdrawableETH[all_users]) <= address(this).balance after every state-changing operation
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy MagicSpend with an owner EOA. Fund it with 10 ETH. 2. Create two CoinbaseSmartWallet accounts (or use the same one). 3. Have MagicSpend owner sign two WithdrawRequests for 8 ETH each with different nonces for the same account. 4. Construct two UserOperations referencing these WithdrawRequests as paymasterAndData. 5. Submit both UserOps via handleOps on the canonical EntryPoint v0.6. 6. Observe that both validatePaymasterUserOp calls succeed. 7. Observe postOp failures (insufficient balance for combined withdrawal). 8. Check _withdrawableETH[account] persists with ~16 ETH credit. 9. Fund MagicSpend with 20 ETH. 10. Call withdrawGasExcess() from the account and verify it extracts the over-credited amount.
uncertainties: Exact EntryPoint v0.6 inner-call gas management could affect whether postOp reverts are handled as expected vs out-of-gas scenarios
Bundler-level checks may reject bundles with multiple UserOps using the same paymaster if they detect balance overlap, but this is not a protocol guarantee
The behavior of Solady's forceSafeTransferETH when balance is insufficient needs verification - it may use create2 fallback which could have different failure modes
additional_testing_needed: Integration test with canonical EntryPoint v0.6 to confirm validation-phase state persistence after postOp failures
Test with varying numbers of concurrent UserOps (2, 5, 10) to confirm linear scaling of over-commitment
Test withdrawGasExcess() after contract replenishment to confirm extraction of unbacked credits
Test whether different senders (not just the same sender) can also create collective over-commitment
